@I322625938715@.title: John Howland
narrative: John Howland (c.1592–1672) is one of the best-documented of all our ancestors — and the source of the family's most famous brush with death. He sailed on the Mayflower in 1620 as a young manservant in the household of Governor John Carver. Somewhere in the storm-tossed mid-Atlantic, Howland was swept overboard into the freezing sea. As William Bradford recorded in his history Of Plymouth Plantation, Howland 'caught hold of the topsail halyards which hung overboard and ran out at length,' clinging on underwater until the crew hauled him back aboard with a boat hook. Had his grip failed, an enormous number of his American descendants — including this family — would simply never have existed.

He signed the Mayflower Compact, survived that first deadly winter, and rose from servant to respected freeman and landowner at Rocky Nook in Kingston, Massachusetts. He married fellow passenger Elizabeth Tilley, and together they had ten children and an estimated eighty-eight grandchildren, making them among the most prolific of all Pilgrim couples. He died in 1672 or early 1673, one of the last surviving Mayflower passengers.

Our link: the Howland line is among the most thoroughly traced of any Pilgrim family, but as with every colonial connection, the generation-by-generation descent down to us should be confirmed against the published Howland genealogies.

@I322625938716@.title: Elizabeth Tilley
narrative: Elizabeth Tilley (1607–1687) crossed the Atlantic on the Mayflower at just thirteen years old, travelling with her parents John and Joan Tilley. Within the first brutal winter at Plymouth she lost both of them — orphaned at fourteen in a half-built settlement on the edge of an unknown continent. She was taken into the household of Governor John Carver.

There she met John Howland, the young servant who had survived being washed overboard during the crossing. They married around 1624 and built one of the largest families in Plymouth Colony — ten children who themselves produced dozens of grandchildren. Elizabeth lived a remarkably long life for the era, dying in Swansea, Massachusetts in December 1687 at about eighty, surrounded by descendants who would eventually number in the millions.
Our link: Elizabeth is a documented first-generation Pilgrim ancestor through the Howland line; confirm each intervening generation against the standard Mayflower descent registers.

@I322625941842@.title: John Tilley
narrative: John Tilley (1571–1621) was a silk-worker from Henlow in Bedfordshire who joined the Mayflower voyage with his wife Joan and their teenage daughter Elizabeth. He was one of the forty-one men who signed the Mayflower Compact in November 1620 — the first framework of self-government written and enacted in what would become the United States.

He did not live to see the colony take root. Like roughly half the passengers, John and Joan Tilley died during the deadly first winter of 1620–21, victims of cold, scurvy, and disease in the makeshift shelters at Plymouth. Their orphaned daughter Elizabeth survived, married John Howland, and carried the Tilley blood forward into countless American families.
Our link: John Tilley reaches us through his daughter Elizabeth Tilley and the Howland line — a documented Mayflower connection worth confirming generation by generation.

@I322738218708@.title: John Alden
narrative: John Alden (c.1598–1687) was hired in Southampton as the Mayflower's cooper — the craftsman responsible for the ship's all-important barrels of food and drink. He was free to return to England once the voyage ended, but he chose to stay and cast his lot with the Pilgrims, signing the Mayflower Compact and becoming one of Plymouth Colony's most durable public servants.

His courtship became American legend. According to tradition immortalized in Henry Wadsworth Longfellow's poem 'The Courtship of Miles Standish,' the captain Myles Standish asked young Alden to propose to Priscilla Mullins on his behalf — only for Priscilla to reply, 'Why don't you speak for yourself, John?' Whatever the truth of the tale, John and Priscilla married around 1621 and had ten children. He served the colony as an assistant governor for more than fifty years and was, at his death in 1687, the last surviving signer of the Mayflower Compact. The Alden House he built in Duxbury still stands today.
Our link: the Alden–Mullins line is one of the most heavily documented in early New England; confirm the descent through the published Alden Kindred genealogies.

@I322738218711@.title: Priscilla Mullins
narrative: Priscilla Mullins (c.1602–after 1651) boarded the Mayflower with her father William, her mother Alice, and her brother Joseph. By the end of the first winter she had lost all three — her entire family wiped out by the sickness that killed half the passengers. Alone at about eighteen, she became one of the small band of survivors who built Plymouth Colony.

She is remembered above all for the line Longfellow gave her: 'Why don't you speak for yourself, John?' — her reported answer when John Alden came courting on behalf of Myles Standish. She married Alden instead, and the couple raised ten children, founding one of the largest and most widely descended families in America. Through their daughters and sons, Priscilla Mullins is an ancestor of U.S. presidents, poets, and a vast number of ordinary American families — including, by tradition, our own.
Our link: Priscilla descends to us through the Alden line; the connection is traditional and well-charted, and merits confirmation against the standard registers.

@I322746646960@.title: Stephen Hopkins
narrative: Stephen Hopkins (c.1581–1644) had already lived one of the most extraordinary lives of any Mayflower passenger before he ever set foot on the ship. In 1609 he sailed for the Virginia colony aboard the Sea Venture, which wrecked on the reefs of Bermuda — the very shipwreck that is widely believed to have inspired William Shakespeare's play The Tempest. Marooned on the island, Hopkins was charged with mutiny for arguing that the castaways were free of authority on shore; he talked his way off a death sentence and eventually reached Jamestown, where he witnessed the colony's terrible 'starving time.'

He returned to England, and in 1620 sailed again — this time on the Mayflower with his wife Elizabeth and children. He was the only Mayflower passenger with prior experience of the New World, which made him invaluable: he served as an interpreter and go-between in the colony's earliest dealings with the Wampanoag, and his servant first encountered the famous Squanto near his household. His son Oceanus was born at sea during the crossing. A signer of the Mayflower Compact, Hopkins ran an inn at Plymouth and remained a colourful, occasionally fined, but indispensable figure until his death in 1644.
Our link: Stephen Hopkins is a documented Mayflower ancestor; trace the descent carefully, as Hopkins has multiple children's lines often confused in family trees.

@I322746646691@.title: William Brewster
narrative: William Brewster (c.1566–1644) was the spiritual and intellectual leader of the Pilgrims. Educated at Cambridge and once a diplomat's assistant at the English court, he became a Separatist — a believer that the true church must break entirely from the Church of England. As postmaster at Scrooby in Nottinghamshire, he turned his manor house into a meeting place for the congregation that would become the Pilgrims, and led them first into exile in Holland and then across the Atlantic.

Aboard the Mayflower he was the senior elder, and in Plymouth Colony — which for years had no ordained minister — Brewster served as its religious teacher and conscience. In England he had run a secret printing press producing Puritan tracts the crown wanted suppressed, and he brought a substantial library to the wilderness. He helped hold the tiny colony together through its hardest years and died in 1644, deeply mourned, having seen Plymouth survive and grow.
Our link: Brewster is a documented Pilgrim elder; his descents are well studied, but confirm the specific line through the published Brewster genealogies.

@I322625933470@.title: Major General Nathaniel Folsom
narrative: Nathaniel Folsom (1726–1790) of Exeter, New Hampshire, was a merchant who became one of his colony's leading Revolutionary figures. He first saw service in the French and Indian War, commanding a New Hampshire company at the Battle of Lake George in 1755. In the years of mounting crisis with Britain he emerged as a patriot leader, and in 1774 New Hampshire sent him as one of its delegates to the First Continental Congress in Philadelphia — the body that began coordinating the colonies' resistance.

He returned to the Continental Congress later in the war and rose to major general of the New Hampshire militia. A signer of the Articles of Association and a fixture of his state's revolutionary government, Folsom bridged the worlds of frontier soldier, frontier merchant, and founding-era statesman. He died in 1790, having lived to see the independent republic he helped bring about.
Our link: General Folsom is a documented public figure of the Revolution; the descent into our New Hampshire / Maine lines is traceable through standard Folsom family records.

@I322738217959@.title: Lady Catherine Carey
narrative: Catherine Carey (c.1524–1569) stood near the very center of the Tudor court. She was the daughter of Mary Boleyn — sister of Anne Boleyn — which made her a first cousin of Queen Elizabeth I. Because Mary Boleyn had been King Henry VIII's mistress before he married Anne, historians have long speculated that Catherine may in fact have been the king's own illegitimate daughter, though this has never been proven.

Whatever her true paternity, Catherine rose high. She married Sir Francis Knollys and became chief Lady of the Bedchamber to Elizabeth I — one of the most trusted women in the queen's inner circle. She bore some fourteen children and was painted by the court artist Steven van der Meulen. When she died in 1569, a grieving Elizabeth gave her a lavish funeral at Westminster Abbey at the crown's own expense.

Family legend — not proof: Catherine Carey appears in our tree as a claimed royal/noble ancestor. The Tudor end of this line is real and richly documented; what remains unproven is the chain of descent connecting her to our verified colonial immigrants. Treat this as a wonderful story to chase down, not an established fact.

@I322625938260@.title: Louis XII, King of France
narrative: Louis XII (1462–1515) reigned as King of France from 1498 until his death, and was known to his subjects as 'the Father of the People' for easing taxes and reforming the courts. Born Louis of Orléans, he came to the throne through the extinction of the senior Valois line, married three times — including, at the end of his life, the young Mary Tudor, sister of Henry VIII — and spent much of his reign waging the Italian Wars in pursuit of claims to Milan and Naples.

His favourite residence was the Château de Blois in the Loire Valley, where the elegant brick-and-stone wing he built still bears his name and his emblem, the crowned porcupine. He rebuilt the castle into one of the finest royal homes in France.

Family legend — not proof: a royal line of descent from Louis XII appears in our tree as a claimed connection. The French royal history is of course beyond doubt; what is unproven is whether a genuine, documented chain links the House of Valois to our colonial ancestors. Enjoy it as family lore worth investigating, not as settled genealogy.

@I322627131190@.title: The Newton Connection
narrative: Our tree carries a claimed connection to the family of Sir Isaac Newton (1643–1727) — the English mathematician and physicist whose laws of motion and universal gravitation reshaped humanity's understanding of the universe. The ancestor recorded here is named as a grandfather of the great scientist, placing our line, by tradition, in the same Lincolnshire Newton family.

Isaac Newton was born on Christmas Day 1642 (old style) at Woolsthorpe Manor near Grantham, the modest stone farmhouse pictured here. It was in its orchard that the famous falling apple is said to have set him thinking about gravity. From those humble surroundings he rose to become a Cambridge professor, president of the Royal Society, Master of the Royal Mint, and one of the most influential scientists who ever lived.

Family legend — not proof: the link to the Newton family is a claimed connection that has not been verified through the colonial bridge. The Newton history is genuine; the descent is the part that needs evidence. A line worth chasing, but not yet established.

@I322738217967@.title: Sir William Carey of Aldenham
narrative: William Carey (c.1500–1528) was a rising star of Henry VIII's court — a Gentleman of the Privy Chamber and Esquire of the Body, positions of great intimacy with the king. He married Mary Boleyn, sister of the future queen Anne Boleyn, and the couple's children (including Catherine Carey) carried the blood of one of England's most consequential families. Mary Boleyn had been the king's mistress, lending the Carey household a permanent air of royal intrigue.

William's promising career was cut brutally short: he died in the sweating sickness epidemic of 1528, still a young man. His widow Mary later fell from favour for marrying a commoner for love, but their descendants — cousins to Elizabeth I — remained close to the throne for generations. The Boleyn family seat at Hever Castle in Kent, shown here, anchors this whole Tudor world.

Family legend — not proof: the Carey/Boleyn line appears in our tree as a claimed noble ancestry. The Tudor figures are historically real and documented; the chain connecting them to our colonial immigrants is what remains unverified. A spectacular story to pursue with proper evidence.

@I322454177849@.title: Sir William Fernald
narrative: Sir William Fernald (recorded 1574–1669) appears in our tree as an early progenitor of the Fernald name and is given the titles of captain and commodore. The Fernald family is firmly rooted in the Piscataqua region — the river boundary between Maine and New Hampshire around Kittery — where Dr. Renald (Reginald) Fernald arrived in the 1630s as surgeon to the early Piscataqua settlement and became the documented immigrant ancestor of nearly all American Fernalds.

The map shown here, drawn around 1670, depicts exactly that stretch of tidal river and the scattered early plantations of Kittery and what is now Portsmouth — the world into which the founding Fernalds of America planted themselves.

Family legend — not proof: the knightly title and the specific English ancestry attached to this William Fernald are claimed rather than proven. The American Fernald line from the Piscataqua is well documented; the leap back to a titled English progenitor is the part that needs hard evidence. Treat the 'Sir' with friendly skepticism until records confirm it.

@I322738217996@.title: Mary Boleyn
narrative: Mary Boleyn (c.1499–1543) is one of the most talked-about women of the Tudor age — the 'other Boleyn girl,' elder sister of Queen Anne Boleyn. As a young woman she served at the French court, and on her return to England she became a mistress of King Henry VIII before he ever set his sights on her sister. Many historians believe her two children, Catherine and Henry Carey, may in fact have been the king's — though Henry never acknowledged them.

Mary married the courtier William Carey, and through their daughter Catherine Carey she became grandmother to a sprawling Elizabethan dynasty. After Carey died of the sweating sickness, Mary scandalised the court by secretly marrying a soldier, William Stafford, purely for love and far beneath her station. Banished from court and cut off by her family, she lived quietly and died in 1543 — outliving her sister Anne and brother George, both executed by the king she had once loved.

Family legend — not proof: Mary Boleyn sits at the head of our claimed Tudor descent, through her daughter Catherine Carey. The Boleyn history is vividly documented; the chain linking it to our colonial ancestors is the unproven part. A glittering story worth chasing with hard evidence.

@I322680433053@.title: Thomas Boleyn, Earl of Wiltshire
narrative: Thomas Boleyn (c.1477–1539) was one of the most accomplished diplomats of Henry VIII's court — a gifted linguist fluent in French and Latin who served as ambassador to the Low Countries and France. From his seat at Hever Castle in Kent he raised a family that would change English history: his daughters Mary and Anne Boleyn, and his son George. When Anne caught the king's eye, Thomas rose to the heights, created Earl of Wiltshire and Ormond.

He became grandfather to Queen Elizabeth I through Anne. But the family's spectacular ascent ended in catastrophe: in 1536 both Anne and George were executed on the king's orders. Thomas survived the purge but lost his offices and his children, dying at Hever just three years later, his dynasty broken.

Family legend — not proof: Thomas Boleyn anchors the claimed Boleyn line in our tree, through his daughter Mary. The Tudor record is beyond doubt; the descent to our family is what still needs proving.

@I322668194797@.title: Thomas Cromwell
narrative: Thomas Cromwell (c.1485–1540) rose from the son of a Putney blacksmith and brewer to become the most powerful man in England after the king — the central figure of Hilary Mantel's 'Wolf Hall.' A brilliant lawyer and administrator, he served as Henry VIII's chief minister and was the chief architect of the English Reformation: he engineered the break with Rome, the king's supremacy over the Church, and the sweeping Dissolution of the Monasteries that redrew the map of English property and power.

Ruthless and astonishingly capable, Cromwell also helped orchestrate the downfall of Anne Boleyn. But he overreached when he arranged Henry's disastrous marriage to Anne of Cleves. His enemies struck; he was arrested, attainted without trial, and beheaded on Tower Hill in 1540 — the king reportedly regretting it almost at once.

Family legend — not proof: a descent from Cromwell's family appears in our tree as a claimed connection. The historical Cromwell is exhaustively documented; the genealogical link to our line is unverified and should be treated as lore until the records bear it out.

@I322625938264@.title: Anne of Brittany
narrative: Anne of Brittany (1477–1514) was a ruler in her own right and one of the most powerful women of her era. As Duchess of Brittany she fought to keep her duchy independent of France, but politics and war made her, in turn, Queen of France twice over — first as the wife of King Charles VIII, and after his death as the wife of King Louis XII. She is the only woman to have been crowned Queen of France on two separate occasions.

Fiercely devoted to Brittany, she negotiated hard to protect its autonomy and is still remembered there as a beloved national figure. Cultured and pious, she presided over a brilliant Renaissance court that moved between the great châteaux of the Loire, including Blois and Amboise. She died in 1514 at thirty-six, and her death effectively sealed Brittany's eventual union with the French crown.

Family legend — not proof: Anne of Brittany appears in our tree as a claimed royal ancestor, linked to the same Valois/French royal line as Louis XII. The French royal history is certain; the descent into our family is the unverified part — a magnificent story to investigate, not a settled fact.

@I322625940390@.title: Sir Thomas Parr of Kendal
narrative: Sir Thomas Parr (c.1483–1517) was a northern English nobleman and trusted courtier of the young Henry VIII, holding lands centred on Kendal Castle in Westmorland. He served as a master of the wards and a companion to the king in the early, glittering years of the reign. His marriage to Maud Green produced children who would reach the very top of Tudor society.

His greatest legacy was his daughter Catherine Parr — the sixth and final wife of Henry VIII, the queen who outlived him, and herself a published author and religious reformer. His son William Parr became Marquess of Northampton. Sir Thomas himself did not live to see any of it; he died young in 1517, probably of the sweating sickness, when Catherine was only a child.
Family legend — not proof: the Parr connection enters our tree as a claimed noble line. The Parrs of Kendal are well documented; the link to our colonial ancestors is the part that awaits proof.

@I322738217958@.title: Sir Francis Knollys
narrative: Sir Francis Knollys (c.1511–1596) was one of the great fixtures of Elizabeth I's government — a committed Puritan who served the queen for decades as Treasurer of the Royal Household and a Privy Councillor. His marriage to Catherine Carey, the queen's cousin, placed the Knollys family at the very centre of court life, and the couple had some fourteen children who married into the highest ranks of the nobility.

Among his more delicate duties, Knollys was made the first custodian of Mary, Queen of Scots after she fled to England in 1568 — a thankless task guarding a rival queen. His daughter Lettice Knollys famously married Robert Dudley, Earl of Leicester, the queen's own favourite, to Elizabeth's lasting fury. Knollys lived into his eighties, dying in 1596 as one of the last links to the early Elizabethan age.

Family legend — not proof: Sir Francis is the husband of our claimed ancestor Catherine Carey. The Knollys family is richly documented in the Tudor record; the descent to our line remains to be confirmed.

@I322669833124@.title: Sir Thomas Smythe — 'The Arranger of Jamestown'
narrative: The Smythe family of Westenhanger in Kent produced one of the great commercial visionaries of the English age of exploration. Sir Thomas Smythe (whose family our tree records here) was the driving organiser behind England's first overseas empires: he served as the first governor of the East India Company and, crucially for American history, as treasurer of the Virginia Company of London — the venture that founded Jamestown in 1607, England's first permanent colony in the New World.

From his counting houses he financed the ships, recruited the colonists, and backed the explorers — including the voyages associated with Captain John Smith and Henry Hudson — that opened North America to English settlement. Without the Smythe family's capital and organisation, the Jamestown that began the English story in America might never have sailed.

Family legend — not proof: this Smythe line appears in our tree as a claimed noble connection, and the recorded dates are tangled (there were several Thomas Smythes across two generations). The Jamestown-founding Smythes are real and documented; the descent to our family is the piece that needs careful evidence.

@I322744489099@.title: Sir John Brydges, 1st Baron Chandos
narrative: Sir John Brydges (1492–1557) was a soldier and royal servant who held one of the most fateful posts in Tudor England: Lieutenant of the Tower of London. It fell to him to guard the nine-days' queen, Lady Jane Grey, through her imprisonment and to escort her to the scaffold in February 1554. By tradition, on the morning of her execution the seventeen-year-old Jane gave Brydges her prayer book as a parting keepsake, inscribed in her own hand.

A loyal supporter of Queen Mary I, Brydges was rewarded for his service with the title Baron Chandos of Sudeley and the great Sudeley Castle in Gloucestershire. He had served the crown as a soldier in France and as a member of Parliament, and died in 1557, founding a noble line that would endure for generations.

Family legend — not proof: Baron Chandos enters our tree as a claimed noble ancestor. The historical figure and his role at the Tower are well documented; the genealogical link to our family is the unverified part.

@I322745357141@.title: Sir John Baker — 'The Bloody Knight of Sissinghurst'
narrative: Sir John Baker (c.1488–1558) climbed to the heights of Tudor government, serving as Attorney General, Chancellor of the Exchequer, and Speaker of the House of Commons under three monarchs. From his grand estate at Sissinghurst in Kent he wielded real power across the reigns of Henry VIII, Edward VI, and Mary I.

It was under the Catholic Queen Mary that he earned the dark nickname history remembers him by — 'Bloody Baker' or 'the Bloody Knight of Sissinghurst' — for his zealous role in the persecution and burning of Protestants. Folklore around Sissinghurst later turned him into an almost ogreish figure of local legend. He died in 1558, the same year as the queen he served.

@I322728996097@.title: Sir Anthony Browne, 1st Viscount Montagu
narrative: Sir Anthony Browne, 1st Viscount Montagu (1528–1592), was one of Elizabethan England's most prominent Catholic noblemen — and a rare survivor. Where most great families bent to the new Protestant settlement, Montagu remained openly, stubbornly Catholic, and yet kept the trust of Queen Elizabeth I through sheer loyalty and discretion. He was the only peer to vote in the House of Lords against the Act of Supremacy that made Elizabeth head of the Church.

A Knight of the Garter, he entertained the queen in spectacular fashion at his palatial home, Cowdray House in Sussex, whose great ruins still stand. He walked a perilous line his whole life, holding to his faith without losing his head — no small feat in an age when Catholic plots brought many of his co-religionists to the block.
Family legend — not proof: Viscount Montagu enters our tree as a claimed noble ancestor. His life is well documented; the link to our family is the unverified piece worth chasing.

@I322668238131@.title: Sir Henry Norris, Baron Norreys of Rycote
narrative: Henry Norris, 1st Baron Norreys (c.1525–1601), carried one of the most poignant names at the Elizabethan court. His father, also Henry Norris, had been a close friend of Henry VIII — and was executed in 1536 as one of the men falsely accused of adultery with Anne Boleyn. The young Henry grew up in the shadow of that judicial murder.

Queen Elizabeth I, perhaps mindful that his father had died in part for loyalty to her mother, showed the family marked favour. She raised Henry to the peerage as Baron Norreys of Rycote, employed him as her ambassador to France, and famously corresponded warmly with him and his wife. Their six soldier sons served the crown across Ireland, France, and the Low Countries, several dying in its wars. He died in 1601, full of honours.
Family legend — not proof: Baron Norreys appears in our tree as a claimed noble line. The historical family is documented; the descent to our ancestors awaits confirmation.

@I322744641558@.title: Oliver of Windleshaw — the Charlemagne Line
narrative: Recorded in our tree as Oliver of Windleshaw of Lancashire, an 'English Immigrant,' this ancestor carries the most ambitious claim of all: a line of descent reaching back to Charlemagne (c.747–814) — Charles the Great, King of the Franks and the first Holy Roman Emperor, who united much of western Europe and was crowned by the Pope on Christmas Day in the year 800.

Such 'descent from Charlemagne' claims are common in old family trees, because virtually everyone of European ancestry alive today is statistically descended from him many times over — the difficulty is proving a specific, documented chain rather than a romantic assertion. The recorded lifespan here (1554–1655, over a hundred years) is itself a sign that this end of the tree needs careful, critical work.

@I322625940387@.title: Lady Anne Parr, Countess of Pembroke
narrative: Anne Parr (1515–1552) achieved something almost no one else at the Tudor court managed: she served as a lady-in-waiting to all six of Henry VIII's wives in turn. The younger sister of Queen Catherine Parr — Henry's sixth and final queen — Anne was a constant, trusted presence in the royal household through every twist of that turbulent reign.

She married William Herbert, a soldier and courtier who rose to become 1st Earl of Pembroke, and through them descend the Earls of Pembroke and the great family seat of Wilton House. The Parrs' own ancestral home was Kendal Castle in Westmorland, now a romantic ruin on a hill above the town. Anne died in 1552 and was buried with honour in St Paul's Cathedral.

@I322625941694@.title: Sir Robert Brooke, Speaker of the Commons
narrative: Sir Robert Brooke (Broke) (died 1558) was one of the foremost lawyers of Tudor England. He rose to be Speaker of the House of Commons in 1554 and then Chief Justice of the Court of Common Pleas — the senior judge of one of England's highest courts. His enduring monument is 'La Graunde Abridgement,' a vast, methodical digest of English case law that lawyers relied on for generations after his death.

With the fortune and standing his career brought, he acquired Madeley Court in Shropshire, the manor that would anchor his family for the next century. He died in 1558, leaving both a legal legacy and a landed dynasty.

@I322625940521@.title: Sir Basil Brooke, Ironmaster
narrative: Sir Basil Brooke (1576–1646) turned the family seat at Madeley into a cradle of the English iron industry. A pioneering ironmaster in the Shropshire coalfield — the very landscape that would later give the world the Iron Bridge and the Industrial Revolution — Brooke held patents for steel-making and ran furnaces and forges along the Severn Gorge.

He was also a devout Catholic and a committed Royalist. During the English Civil War his loyalty to King Charles I cost him dearly: his works were seized and he was imprisoned by Parliament for alleged intrigues on the king's behalf. He died in 1646 as the royal cause collapsed, his Madeley Court still standing today (now restored as a hotel).

@I322625939928@.title: Captain Edward Johnson, Chronicler of New England
narrative: Edward Johnson (1598–1672) was one of the founding fathers of Woburn, Massachusetts — and the author of the first published history of the Massachusetts Bay Colony. An English immigrant who came over in the great Puritan migration, he helped lay out the new town of Woburn in the 1640s, drafted its founding 'town orders,' and served for decades as its town clerk and as a deputy (representative) to the Massachusetts General Court.

In 1654 he published, in London, 'A History of New England' — better known by its ringing title 'The Wonder-Working Providence of Sion's Saviour in New England.' It is the earliest general history of the colony written by a settler who lived it, a vivid, providential account of how the Puritans built their 'city on a hill' out of the wilderness. He also captained the Woburn militia, making him captain, founder, lawmaker, and historian all at once.
Our link: Edward Johnson is a documented early New England ancestor; trace the line through the well-published Johnson and Woburn genealogies to confirm each generation.

@I322680557012@.title: Captain Thomas Bradbury & the Salem Witch Trials
narrative: Thomas Bradbury (1611–1695) was a leading citizen of Salisbury, Massachusetts — a magistrate, militia captain, and clerk who held nearly every town office over a long life on the frontier of the Bay Colony. But the Bradbury name is remembered above all for what happened to his wife.

In 1692, at the height of the Salem witchcraft hysteria, his elderly wife Mary Bradbury was accused of witchcraft, tried, and condemned to hang. More than a hundred neighbours signed petitions testifying to her good character — a rare show of community courage in that terrifying year. Whether through their efforts, the delays of her family, or sheer luck, Mary escaped execution and outlived the panic, dying peacefully in 1700. Thomas had died in 1695, spared the worst of the ordeal.
Our link: the Bradburys of Salisbury are documented ancestors; the Salem trial records and Bradbury genealogies allow the line to be traced and verified.

@I322454177822@.title: Dr. Renald Fernald — the First Fernald in America
narrative: Dr. Renald Fernald (also written Reginald or Regnald, c.1595–1656) is the immigrant progenitor of the American Fernald family — the ancestor from whom essentially every Fernald in this tree descends. He appears in the original list of “Stewards and Servants sent by John Mason” as “Reginald Furnald, Chirurgeon” (surgeon). An English surgeon, he came to the Piscataqua region of New England around 1630–1631 in connection with Captain John Mason's colonising venture, the Laconia Company, the enterprise behind the settlement at Strawbery Banke — today's Portsmouth, New Hampshire. (His English origins and a tradition of prior service as a naval surgeon are family lore rather than documented fact.)

Fernald is consistently remembered as the first physician and surgeon among the New Hampshire settlers, practising as a “chirurgeon” at the fledgling Piscataqua plantation. He also became a pillar of the colony's civic life: the records show him as a grand juror (1634), a commissioner for the trial of minor cases (1649), and clerk and recorder of the courts and of deeds (1654) up to his death — as well as a surveyor.

He held land on an island in the Piscataqua River long known by his name; Fernald's Island stayed in the family until 1806, when it passed to the federal government and became part of the Portsmouth Naval Shipyard. His wife was named Joanna (the surname “Warburton” is traditional but unverified), and the couple had about seven children — among them sons William, who settled in Kittery, Maine, and John — through whom the family multiplied across New England.

Renald Fernald died in 1656 near Great Island (New Castle) and is said to be buried at the Point of Graves burying ground in Portsmouth, across the river from his home. The broad outline of his life is well supported; the details of his English birth, his wife's maiden name, and the exact ship he arrived on are best treated as tradition. By descent he is the common ancestor of the American Fernalds — popularly said to include figures as varied as Robert Frost and Stephen King.

@I322454177539@.title: The Fernalds of Kittery & the Shipbuilders of the Piscataqua
narrative: The Fernald family's roots on the Piscataqua reach back to the first English settlement of the region. Dr. Renald Fernald arrived around 1631 as surgeon to Captain John Mason's company at Strawbery Banke (later Portsmouth, New Hampshire). His son William Fernald (c.1646–1728) settled across the river at Spruce Creek in Kittery, Maine — he married Elizabeth Langdon, served as a Kittery selectman, was commissioned a militia lieutenant in 1686, and is styled “Captain” in town records by the 1690s (Stackpole, Old Kittery and Her Families, pp.376–377). From this colonial planter and militia officer the Fernalds grew into a long-lived Kittery and Portsmouth family.

Two centuries later the Fernald name became famous in shipbuilding — though, to set the record straight, the shipbuilder was Frederick W. Fernald, not a William. Around 1837 Frederick took over the historic Badger's Island shipyard in Kittery, directly across the Piscataqua from Portsmouth, and in 1844 partnered with master builder William Pettigrew to form Fernald & Pettigrew. Badger's Island was storied ground: earlier builders there included James Hackett, who built the Continental Navy's USS Ranger and the frigate America during the Revolution, and William Badger, who launched well over 100 vessels.

Fernald & Pettigrew became one of the four great Piscataqua yards of the clipper era, building some thirty vessels — eight of them clipper ships — in the boom of 1851–1855. Their masterpiece was the clipper Typhoon, launched fully rigged in 1851; on her maiden run she crossed from Portsmouth to Liverpool in about 13½ days and arrived the largest ship the port had yet seen, earning the nickname the “Portsmouth Flyer.” Other Fernald & Pettigrew clippers — Dashing Wave, Water Witch, Express, Midnight, Noonday — carried the Gold Rush trade round Cape Horn to San Francisco and the China tea routes. For a generation the Fernald name stood among the finest builders of fast sailing ships in America.

Family note: the direct, generation-by-generation line from this Captain William Fernald (1646–1728) down to the shipbuilder Frederick W. Fernald is consistent with the family's deep Kittery/Portsmouth presence but is not proven link-by-link here — treat it as well-founded family tradition pending confirmation in the Fernald and NEHGS records.

@I322758507983@.title: The Winstons of Virginia
narrative: Capt. Isaac Winston is the traditional immigrant patriarch named in genealogies of the Winston family of colonial Virginia, said to have come from Dorset, England before the family settled in Hanover County. The earliest English generations are uncertain and inconsistent across sources, so the Dorset origin should be read as family tradition rather than proven fact.

The Winstons became a prominent Hanover County planter family with two famous descendants: Sarah Winston was the mother of the patriot orator Patrick Henry (“give me liberty, or give me death”), and the same Winston line connects to First Lady Dolley Payne Madison. Those descents are well established; the specific tie back to a 16th-century Dorset Winston is the legend-prone part of the chain.

@I322625933704@.title: Philip Philipse & the Lords of Philipsburg Manor
narrative: Philip Philipse (1663–1699) was the eldest son and intended heir of Frederick Philipse, the Dutch-born merchant who became 1st Lord of Philipsburg Manor and one of the wealthiest men in colonial New York; his mother was the formidable trader Margaret Hardenbroeck. Philip was sent to manage the family's sugar estate in Barbados, where he died in 1699, predeceasing his father.

Because Philip died first, the great Hudson Valley manor passed in part to his son, Frederick Philipse II, who carried on the line. The family's seats — Philipse Manor Hall in Yonkers and Philipsburg Manor in Sleepy Hollow — both still stand as historic sites today.

@I322638416822@.title: Capt. Joseph Ives of New Haven
narrative: Capt. Joseph Ives (1647–1694) was born in New Haven, Connecticut, son of William Ives, one of the original settlers of the New Haven Colony. In 1672 he married Mary Yale — a first cousin of Elihu Yale, the namesake of Yale University — and the couple raised ten children. He was among the first settlers of the North Haven area and a signer of the 1668 Wallingford Agreement.

Joseph served as captain of the local train-band (militia), and tradition holds that before a meeting house was built, neighbors gathered at the Ives home for Sabbath worship. He died in 1694 and is buried in North Haven.

@I322728992483@.title: Ensign Walter Fairfield of Wenham
narrative: Ensign Walter Fairfield (1631–1723) was an early settler of Wenham in Essex County, Massachusetts. Baptized in 1631 at Copford, Essex, England, son of John Fairfield, he emigrated to New England and married Sarah in 1654. He lived a remarkably long life, dying at Wenham in 1723 “in his 92nd year.”

A farmer and tavern keeper, Walter was a pillar of his small town — serving on a Salem county-court jury, as constable and treasurer, as selectman and repeatedly as moderator of Town Meeting, and as a representative to the General Court in 1692. His title “Ensign” came from service in the town militia. (The Fairfields connect to our Bagley side through Emma Fairfield, mother of Ethel Pierce.)

@I322743744992@.title: Sir Thomas Bacon of Helmingham
narrative: The Bacons were a widespread gentry family in Tudor and Stuart Suffolk, and a Thomas Bacon recorded as born at Helmingham on 31 May 1566 appears in genealogies as dying in 1614 at nearby Winston, Suffolk. Helmingham itself is best known for Helmingham Hall, the moated brick house begun by the Tollemache family around 1480; the Bacons of this district were neighbours rather than owners of the Hall. The Suffolk Bacons produced several distinguished branches, and family tradition links this Thomas to the most famous of them.

It is important to separate fact from family legend. The celebrated Sir Nicholas Bacon, Lord Keeper under Elizabeth I, and his son the philosopher Sir Francis Bacon descended from the Bacons of Drinkstone and Redgrave — a line documented separately from the Helmingham Bacons. No reliable record establishes a close kinship between Sir Thomas Bacon of Helmingham and the Lord Keeper or Sir Francis Bacon, and our descent from this Bacon line into the colonial American family is itself unproven family legend.

@I322616879206@.title: Lady Alice Gage of Firle
narrative: Alice Gage was a daughter of Sir John Gage (1479–1556) of Firle, Sussex — the powerful Tudor courtier who served Henry VIII as Vice-Chamberlain and Constable of the Tower of London — and his wife Philippa Guildford. The Gages had held Firle since the fifteenth century, and Firle Place remains the family seat to this day. About 1525 Alice married Sir Anthony Browne, K.G., Master of the Horse; she died in 1540, and her son became the 1st Viscount Montagu.

The Gage family of Firle is genuinely distinguished, and its most famous descendant was General Thomas Gage (1719–1787), British commander-in-chief in North America at the outbreak of the Revolution at Lexington and Concord in 1775. Our line's descent from this noble Gage family is, however, claimed family legend rather than documented fact.

@I322710655849@.title: Sir William Acton, Ironmaster of Aldenham
narrative: William Acton belonged to the Acton family of Aldenham, near Morville in Shropshire — a gentry line later raised to the Acton baronetcy and ultimately to the Barons Acton (including the Victorian historian Lord Acton). Beyond his landholding he was an early ironmaster: he ran iron mills at Morville and in 1561 was licensed to fell timber in Shirlett Forest to fuel his works, part of the rise of the Shropshire iron industry.

The records are tangled, and dates conflict: the History of Parliament records a William Acton of Aldenham (by 1513–1567) who sat as MP for Bridgnorth in 1554 and 1555, and genealogies giving a 1497 birth may conflate him with a relative. As with our other English noble connections, the link from this Shropshire Acton family into our colonial American line is claimed family legend, not established by documentary proof.

@I322744510266@.title: Baroness Chandos of Sudeley
narrative: Elizabeth Grey was a daughter of Edmund Grey, 9th Baron Grey de Wilton. About 1512 she married John Brydges (1492–1557), the soldier-courtier who served Henry VIII, Edward VI, and Mary I and who was Lieutenant of the Tower of London during Lady Jane Grey's imprisonment. In 1554 Queen Mary granted Brydges Sudeley Castle and created him Baron Chandos of Sudeley; Elizabeth thus became the first Baroness Chandos. The couple had eleven children; she died in December 1559.

Sudeley Castle, the family's new seat, was already famous as the home and burial place of Queen Katherine Parr, Henry VIII's last wife, who died there in 1548. The Brydges/Chandos barony is well documented; what remains family legend in our case is the line of descent connecting this noble Chandos family to our colonial ancestors.

@I322454173952@.title: Capt. Nathaniel Woodbridge & Anne Bradstreet
narrative: Nathaniel Woodbridge was a member of the prominent Woodbridge family of Newbury, Massachusetts — one of the leading clerical and civic dynasties of early New England. The family was established in America by the Rev. John Woodbridge (1613–1695), who came from Wiltshire, settled at Newbury as its first town clerk, and helped found Andover. He married Mercy Dudley, daughter of Governor Thomas Dudley and sister of the poet Anne Bradstreet.

The Woodbridge name carries an outsized place in literary history: it was Rev. John Woodbridge who carried the manuscript of his sister-in-law Anne Bradstreet's poems to London, where in 1650 they were published as 'The Tenth Muse Lately Sprung Up in America' — the first published book of poetry by a North American woman. Nathaniel (1696–1731) belonged to the later Newbury generations of this family; his exact placement in the pedigree is best confirmed against 'The Woodbridge Record.'

@I322625937167@.title: Lt. John Folsom of Exeter
narrative: The Folsom family of New Hampshire descends from John Folsom (Foulsham), who emigrated in 1638 with his wife Mary Gilman from Hingham in Norfolk, England, settling first at Hingham, Massachusetts and then removing to Exeter, New Hampshire about 1655. The surname comes from the village of Foulsham in Norfolk. Lieut. John Folsom (1685–1740) of Exeter belonged to the third American generation of this well-rooted Exeter family.

The Folsoms produced notable descendants: General Nathaniel Folsom (1726–1790), a New Hampshire militia commander and delegate to the Continental Congress, and — most famously — Frances Folsom Cleveland (1864–1947), who in 1886 married President Grover Cleveland in a White House ceremony and, at 21, became the youngest First Lady in American history.

@I322625937722@.title: Capt. Benjamin Tibbetts of Dover
narrative: Benjamin Tibbetts was born in Dover, New Hampshire, on 31 October 1700, a son of Henry Tibbetts. The Tibbetts (Tibbets/Tebbetts) family was one of the founding families of Dover, settled on Dover Neck and Dover Point from the seventeenth century. Several Tibbetts kinsmen maintained fortified 'garrison houses' — strongholds that protected settlers during the frontier wars between English colonists and the Abenaki and their French allies.

Benjamin married before 1725 and later relocated to Lebanon, in York County, Maine, where he died on 30 June 1761. His life spans the transition of the Tibbetts family from the dangerous Oyster River / Dover frontier of the 1690s and early 1700s toward the steady expansion of settlement into southern Maine.

@I322454173978@.title: Capt. John Pearson & the First Fulling Mill
narrative: John Pearson of Rowley descended from Deacon John Pearson, who came from Yorkshire to New England about 1637 and settled at Rowley, Massachusetts. In 1643 Deacon John built and operated what is traditionally celebrated as the first fulling mill in colonial America, in the part of Rowley that became Byfield. A fulling mill cleansed and thickened woven woolen cloth; for roughly fifty years it was the only one in eastern Massachusetts.

The Pearsons became one of Rowley's leading families — the original Deacon John served as selectman, assessor, and Rowley's deputy to the Massachusetts General Court. Capt. John Pearson (1690–1781) carried the family's standing into the eighteenth century. The founding mill marks the family's place in the story of America's earliest textile industry.

@I322745909310@.title: Capt. Victor Putman of Schenectady
narrative: Victor (Davidse) Putman lived at Schenectady in the Province of New York from about 1680 into the 1750s and belonged to the Dutch Putman (Poutman) family of the upper Hudson and Mohawk valleys. The family stemmed from Jan Putman, an Albany merchant born in Holland about 1645; Jan and his wife were killed in the French and Indian massacre that burned Schenectady on 8 February 1690. Victor married Grietje (sister of Jan Pieterse Mabie) at Albany in December 1706, and the family went on to populate Schenectady and the neighbouring counties.

A candid note on the famous name: this Schenectady Putman family is a Dutch lineage (Poutman/Putman) and is NOT the same as — nor demonstrably connected to — the English Putnam family of Salem, Massachusetts that produced General Israel 'Old Put' Putnam. The similar spelling is coincidental; no credible evidence links Victor Putman of Schenectady to the New England Putnams.

@I322475441013@.title: Capt. John Bragg of Rehoboth
narrative: John Bragg (born about 1659) is an early progenitor of the Bragg family of New England, from whom our Braggs who settled Albion, Maine, descend. He appears in the records at Rehoboth, in Bristol County, Massachusetts, where on 12 October 1684 he married Elizabeth Millard; their children included Thomas, Katherine, and Experience Bragg. Rehoboth was a frontier farming town on the Massachusetts–Rhode Island border, and the Braggs were among its working settler families.

Family tradition describes John as English-born, but this should be flagged as uncertain: his parentage and birthplace are not documented. The careful conclusion is that John Bragg is a genuine, documented early New England ancestor of the Bragg line, while his specific English origins remain unproven.

@I322454172153@.title: Ensign Samuel Adams of Kittery
narrative: Samuel Adams of Kittery in York County, Maine (then part of the Province of Massachusetts Bay) appears in genealogical records as born about 1710 and dying around 1775, remembered in family tradition as a militia ensign — a junior commissioned officer who typically carried the company colors. Kittery, Maine's oldest town, was a shipbuilding and seafaring community on the Piscataqua across from Portsmouth, and like every New England town it maintained a trained militia in which most able-bodied men served. Documentation for this Samuel Adams rests largely on compiled genealogies rather than a definitive primary source, so the specifics of his service are modestly supported rather than firmly proven.

It is important to state plainly that this Samuel Adams is NOT the famous Boston patriot and Founding Father Samuel Adams (1722–1803). They share only a very common English surname; the Boston Samuel Adams was born to a different family and is exhaustively documented, while the Kittery Samuel Adams is an unrelated Maine townsman of the same name. Any claim of kinship to the patriot should be regarded as a coincidence of naming.

@I322454173512@.title: Joseph Thomes & the Burning of Falmouth
narrative: Joseph Thomes was a member of the Thomes family of Falmouth, Maine (the part of old Falmouth that is now Portland), born around 1735 and dying about 1778. The Thomes surname appears repeatedly in early Falmouth town and church records, and a Joseph Thomes is listed among Falmouth's training-band (militia) soldiers in a 1757 muster — placing the family among the working settlers of lower Casco Bay in the mid-1700s.

Joseph lived through one of the most dramatic moments in his town's history. On 18 October 1775, early in the Revolution, a Royal Navy squadron under Captain Henry Mowat bombarded and burned Falmouth Neck, destroying most of its public buildings and about two-thirds of its houses — an attack that outraged the colonies and helped spur the creation of an American navy. His reported death around 1778 falls within these turbulent war years; the rebuilt settlement was incorporated as Portland in 1786.

@I322454173560@.title: John Crooks of Ipswich
narrative: John Crooks is identified as a resident of Ipswich, Essex County, Massachusetts, born about 1717 and dying about 1783, who served as a private during the Revolutionary era. Ipswich was one of the oldest and most prosperous towns of the Massachusetts Bay Colony, settled in 1634 and famous today for its concentration of surviving First Period (17th-century) timber-framed houses. Like most New England towns it fielded militia companies, and ordinary men served as privates in the local trained bands.

Very little is firmly documented about this particular John Crooks; the surname is uncommon in Ipswich records, and searches turned up no strong primary-source confirmation beyond the compiled family tree. The honest assessment is that he was most likely a minor figure — an ordinary townsman and rank-and-file soldier of the Revolution who left only a faint documentary trace; claims about his service should be stated cautiously until corroborated.

@I322638419526@.title: The Strouts of Cape Elizabeth
narrative: The Strouts were a well-established family of Cape Elizabeth and the surrounding Cumberland County coast of Maine in the 1700s — a region of fishermen, mariners, and small farmers on the rocky shores south of Falmouth/Portland. Family records place a Joshua Strout in this line, remembered with the title “Captain,” which in coastal Maine could mean either a militia captain or a master mariner, both common among the seafaring Strout men.

Sources disagree on his exact dates: some show a Joshua Strout (1722–1765) and a Joshua Strout Jr. (1745–1793) who served in the 2nd Regiment of Cumberland County Militia during the Revolution and reportedly died in the British West Indies in 1793. The particulars are reconstructed from compiled trees and should be treated as tentative; what is solid is the family's deep, multigenerational roots on the Cape Elizabeth coast and its members' service in the militia and maritime trades.

@I322638416581@.title: Capt. David Ives of Wallingford
narrative: David Ives was born 15 June 1740 in Wallingford, New Haven County, Connecticut, a son of Benjamin Ives and Hannah Moss, and died about 1820. He belonged to the Ives family long associated with the New Haven Colony and the inland town of Wallingford, settled in 1670. Genealogical and Sons of the American Revolution records identify him as a Revolutionary War patriot — one account has him responding as a corporal to the Lexington Alarm of April 1775 and later rising to lieutenant and captain, the rank by which he is remembered.

Some sources associate an officer named David Ives with Colonel Seth Warner's regiment (the Green Mountain Boys) on the New York/Vermont frontier, though it is not certain this is the same Wallingford man, and his death year varies between 1815 and 1820. The core facts — his 1740 Wallingford birth into the Ives family and his Revolutionary service — are reasonably supported, while the precise unit and rank history should be treated with some caution.

@I322727233107@.title: Capt. Thomas Rose of New London
narrative: Thomas Rose was born 10 September 1653 in New London, Connecticut, and died in 1743/44 in New London County. He married Hannah Allyn, daughter of Robert and Sarah Allyn, in 1680 at Stonington. Among the better-documented of these ancestors, he served in King Philip's War (1675–76) and shared in the subsequent division of land, was a substantial landholder, and represented Preston as a deputy to the Connecticut General Assembly in 1695 and again from 1720 to 1724. His estate inventory at death was valued at nearly £2,500 — a wealthy man for his time and place.

He is called “Sergeant” in some records and “Captain” in others, reflecting militia service in the towns around New London, one of colonial Connecticut's principal seaports; a great oak near his house served as a noted boundary marker between Norwich and New London. As an early settler, soldier, landholder, and elected deputy, Thomas Rose left a clearer documentary footprint than many in this group.

@I322625940341@.title: The Blayneys of Gregynog
narrative: The Blayney family of Gregynog were genuine Welsh gentry: from roughly the 15th century until the death of the bachelor Arthur Blayney in 1795, Gregynog (near Newtown, Montgomeryshire, now Powys) was their principal seat. The family produced sheriffs of Montgomeryshire and barristers of the Inner Temple, and proudly traced a lineage back toward the early Welsh princes — even, by tradition, to the semi-legendary Caradog Fraichfras. A Thomas Blayney of this house in the 1500s–1600s is plausible, though records of that period blend documented pedigree with heraldic family tradition.

For our family, the descent from the Blayneys down to the colonial New England line should be treated as claimed family legend rather than proven fact. Connecting an emigrant ancestor to a specific gentry house in Britain is one of the most error-prone leaps in genealogy, frequently built on surname coincidence. The Blayneys of Gregynog were real and notable; the chain linking them to us is the unverified part — a colourful tradition pending documentary proof.

@I322741257998@.title: The Barkers of Sibton
narrative: Sibton, in Suffolk, is best known for Sibton Abbey, the only Cistercian monastery founded in East Anglia (1150, dissolved 1536), whose refectory ruins still stand. A family recorded there in the 1500s under the name “Barker,” sometimes given as “Chapman alias Barker,” is genuinely attested: a memorial brass in Sibton church commemorates an Edmund Chapman alias Barker with his wife and many children, anchoring the family to that parish in the Tudor period. Genealogies place an Edmund Barker of Sibton roughly 1540–1626, though the dates and even the “Captain” title vary, and the military rank is not well supported.

As with the other gentry lines, the descent from this Suffolk Barker family to our colonial ancestors should be framed as claimed family legend. The Sibton Barker/Chapman family existed and is partly documented through parish records and the church brass, but the links carrying that line across the Atlantic to our tree are the unverified portion — a real Tudor Suffolk family and an attractive tradition, but no firm proof of the connection.

@I322669832503@.title: Christopher Baker of Tenterden
narrative: Christopher Baker is placed by family tradition in Tenterden, Kent — a prosperous Wealden market town whose medieval wealth came from wool and cloth, with a fine church (St Mildred's) and a High Street still lined with timber-framed and weatherboarded houses. Genealogies give his birth as about 1528 in Tenterden, a death around 1625–1628, and a marriage to Dorothy Thompson; some accounts colourfully add that he commanded a ship against the Spanish Armada in 1588. The Baker surname is well attested in Tudor Kent, so a Christopher Baker of Tenterden is plausible, but the details rest on compiled trees rather than verified primary sources.

Two cautions are essential. First, the reported lifespan of roughly 100 years (c.1528–c.1628) is highly doubtful — far above normal life expectancy and a classic sign that two or more namesakes have been conflated; sources variously end his life in 1616, 1625, or 1628. Second, like the Welsh and Suffolk lines, the descent from this Kentish Baker family down to our colonial ancestors should be treated as claimed family legend, not fact, until confirmed against parish registers.

@I322625937930@.title: The “Indian Princess” Family Legend
narrative: This ancestor embodies one of the most common motifs in American family lore: the supposed descent from a Native American “Indian princess.” Such stories were repeated in countless families through the 19th and 20th centuries, very often attached to a Cherokee or generic “Indian” woman, and they are almost never supported by documentation. Genealogists and Native scholars note that the trope is largely a myth: the Cherokee and most other tribes had no hereditary “princesses,” and the romantic image was popularized by dime novels, Wild West shows, and Pocahontas-style folklore rather than by family records. The label “Sandusky” itself looks like a place-name (the Ohio region) attached to a person — a classic sign of a garbled or invented entry.

Treat this purely as family legend. There is no verified evidence that an actual Native American woman of this name and dates joins our line, and the claim should not be presented as fact. Where such stories have any kernel of truth, it usually traces to a beloved grandmother, a misremembered surname, or a geographic association rather than tribal ancestry. We flag this entry as dubious and unproven. The accompanying image is a period engraving of Pocahontas, included only to illustrate how the “Indian princess” figure was imagined in early European art — not as a portrait of this ancestor.

sources.title: Why Your Great-Grandmother Wasn't A Cherokee Princess — Native Languages of the Americas
The 'Cherokee Princess' in Your Family Tree — Genealogy Explained

@I322738218157@.title: The Spencers — Diana & Churchill
narrative: The claim here ties our family to the Spencers of England, one of the most prominent aristocratic houses in British history. The family was established at Althorp in Northamptonshire by the early 16th century — Sir John Spencer built Althorp House around 1508 — and the line later rose to the earldoms of Sunderland and Spencer and the dukedom of Marlborough. The Spencers are most famous today for two descendants: Sir Winston Spencer-Churchill (the Churchills became “Spencer-Churchill”) and Diana, Princess of Wales, who grew up at Althorp and is buried there.

While the Spencer family is thoroughly historical, our descent from it is family legend and is not established. The connection passes through the unproven “colonial bridge” linking American colonists back to English nobility, a stretch rarely supported by primary records and often relying on hopeful 19th-century pedigrees. “Margaret Eleanor Spencer (1472–1536)” should be treated with caution, as early Spencer women of this generation are sparsely documented. An attractive but unverified tradition, not fact.

@I322746016860@.title: The De La Warrs — & the Name “Delaware”
narrative: This ancestor is placed within the De La Warr (West) family — the English noble house from which the name “Delaware” ultimately derives. The barony De La Warr was first created in 1299; its most famous member was Thomas West, 3rd Baron De La Warr (1576–1618), colonial governor of Virginia. In 1610 Captain Samuel Argall named Delaware Bay in his honor, and the Delaware River, the Delaware (Lenape) people, and eventually the U.S. state of Delaware all took their names from that bay.

The De La Warr / West family is real and well recorded, but our personal descent from it is family legend, not proven fact. The entry itself is uncertain — the dual given name “William (Richard)” and the title “Lord of Isfield” suggest a garbled or conflated record. As with the other noble claims, the chain back through the colonial period is unverified. The image is a 19th-century engraved portrait of Thomas West, 3rd Baron De La Warr, included to illustrate the line and the Delaware naming connection.

@I322625941889@.title: The Holland Earls of Kent
narrative: This ancestor is attached to the Holland (de Holand) family, a medieval English noble house that rose from Lancashire knightly gentry to the highest ranks of the realm. Their fortunes turned on the marriage of Sir Thomas Holland to Joan Plantagenet, the “Fair Maid of Kent,” granddaughter of King Edward I. Through that union the Hollands became Earls of Kent, and later Dukes of Surrey and Exeter, entangled with the Plantagenet dynasty during the reigns of Richard II and the Wars of the Roses.

The Holland Earls of Kent are firmly historical, but our descent from them is family legend and remains unproven. The dates here (c.1535–1595) fall well after the main medieval Holland earls (14th–15th centuries), so this “Lady Margaret de Holland” is likely a later or conflated figure; the surname alone does not establish noble descent. We present it honestly as tradition. The image is the heraldic achievement of Thomas Holland, 2nd Earl of Kent — the royal arms of Edward I within a silver border.

@I322625941817@.title: The Beauchamps, Earls of Warwick
narrative: This ancestor is linked to the Beauchamp family, the medieval Earls of Warwick and one of the great baronial houses of England. The line's most celebrated member, Richard Beauchamp, 13th Earl of Warwick (1382–1439), served Henry IV, V, and VI and was a principal commander in the conquest of Normandy; his gilt-bronze effigy in the Beauchamp Chapel at St Mary's, Warwick, is regarded as the finest 15th-century English bronze. The Warwick title later passed by marriage to the Nevilles, producing Richard Neville, the “Kingmaker.”

The Beauchamp earls are thoroughly documented, but our descent from them is legend and is not established. “Sir Thomas Beauchamp (1550–1613)” sits long after the medieval Beauchamp earls (whose earldom male line ended in the 15th century), so the entry is likely a later namesake or hopeful attribution. The image shows the effigy of Richard Beauchamp, presented to illustrate the historic family rather than this ancestor.

@I322625941709@.title: The Grosvenors — Dukes of Westminster
narrative: This ancestor is connected to the Grosvenor family — the Cheshire house that became the Dukes of Westminster, among the wealthiest families in Britain. The Grosvenors (the name means “chief huntsman”) acquired the Eaton estate in Cheshire by marriage in the 1440s, where Eaton Hall remains their seat. Their later fortune came from the development of London's Mayfair and Belgravia; the dukedom of Westminster was created by Queen Victoria in 1874.

The Grosvenor family is real and exceptionally well documented, but our descent from it is family legend and is not proven. “Margaret Grosvenor of Shropshire (1485–1558)” is plausibly of the broader Cheshire/Shropshire Grosvenor gentry, yet there is no verified line connecting her to our tree. As with the other noble claims, the link has not been demonstrated through the colonial bridge. The image is the arms of the Duke of Westminster (Grosvenor).

@I322744634747@.title: The Greys — Lady Jane Grey's Line
narrative: Family legend connects this ancestor to the Grey family, one of the great houses of late-medieval and Tudor England. The Greys descended from an Anglo-Norman line and were ennobled from the 13th century as Barons Grey of Codnor, of Ruthyn, and of Wilton, later rising to viscounts, earls, marquesses, and dukes. The family famously produced Lady Jane Grey, the “Nine Days' Queen,” great-granddaughter of Henry VII, proclaimed queen in July 1553 and executed for treason in February 1554; the Greys also intermarried with the Hastings earls of Huntingdon — the union this entry's name evokes.

The Grey family is real and well documented, but our descent from it through the colonial bridge is unproven and should be treated as family legend, not fact. The dates here (1463–1511) and the compound name “Grey de Hastings” do not correspond to a securely identified individual in the standard genealogies of either family, so the specific person is uncertain or garbled. We honor the tradition while flagging that the link has not been verified.

@I322625938714@.title: Warwick Castle & the Beauchamps
narrative: Family legend ties this ancestor to the Beauchamp family, historically the Earls of Warwick. The Beauchamps held the earldom and Warwick Castle for roughly 180 years from 1267, when the castle passed to William de Beauchamp, 9th Earl of Warwick. Successive Beauchamp earls — Guy de Beauchamp (who captured Piers Gaveston in 1312) and Thomas de Beauchamp (who built Caesar's Tower and Guy's Tower) — transformed the medieval fortress into one of England's grandest strongholds. The direct Beauchamp male line ended in 1449, after which the title passed to Richard Neville, the “Kingmaker.”

The Beauchamp earls of Warwick are real and prominent in English history, but our descent from them is not established and should be treated as legend. The stated dates (1581–1674) fall more than a century after the Beauchamp earldom had passed out of the family, and “Dorothy Kent Beauchamp” does not match a documented member of the comital house, so this entry likely conflates a common surname with the noble dynasty.

@I322728004471@.title: The Grosvenors of Cheshire
narrative: Family legend connects this ancestor to the Grosvenor family of Cheshire, later the Dukes of Westminster. The Grosvenors traditionally trace themselves to Gilbert le Grosvenor, said to have come with William the Conqueror, and were long a leading Cheshire county family seated at Eaton. They are famous in heraldic history for Scrope v Grosvenor (1385–1390), in which Sir Robert Grosvenor lost the right to bear one coat and afterward adopted “Azure, a garb or” — the golden wheatsheaf — which his descendants, the Dukes of Westminster, still use.

The Grosvenor family of Cheshire is real and well documented, but our descent from it across the colonial bridge is unproven and should be regarded as legend. @I322625941768@.title: The de Vaux — an Anglo-Norman Line
narrative: Family legend links this ancestor to the de Vaux (Vaus / de Vallibus) family, an old Anglo-Norman house recorded from the Norman Conquest. Robert and Aitard de Vaux are said to have come in the retinue of Roger Bigod and gained lands in Norfolk, and the name appears in Domesday Book (1086) as “de Vals” and “de Vallibus.” Hubert de Vaux received the barony of Gilsland in Cumberland — the branch whose arms, “Chequy or and gules” (a gold-and-red checkerboard), are the best known. The surname spread into Scotland and Ireland as Vans, Vance, and Vaus.

The de Vaux family is a genuine Anglo-Norman lineage, but our descent from it is unproven and should be treated as legend. “Alice de Vaux (1482–1543)” cannot be tied to a documented member of the Gilsland or other principal de Vaux lines, and the surname's many spelling variants make misattribution easy. The Gilsland arms stand for the historical family, not as evidence of our connection.

@I322746016819@.title: The Warres of Hestercombe
narrative: Family legend connects this ancestor to the Warre family of Somerset, long seated at Hestercombe near Taunton. Hestercombe came to the Warres in 1391, when Elizabeth de Meriet, heiress of Sir John de Meriet, married John la Warre, and the family held the estate for almost 500 years until it was sold to the 1st Viscount Portman in 1872. The Warres were established gentry across Devon, Kent, Sussex, Gloucestershire, and Somerset; a Warre baronetcy of Hestercombe was created in 1673 for Sir Francis Warre, MP.

The Warre family of Hestercombe is real and well documented, but our descent from it is unproven and should be treated as family legend. The forename Dionysia is plausible for the period, but no specifically documented “Dionysia Warre” matching this entry has been verified in the Hestercombe pedigree, so the individual is uncertain. The image of Hestercombe House is offered as the family's historic seat, not as proof of kinship.

@I322454166508@.title: Daniel Meservey of Morrill
narrative: Daniel Smith Meservey was born November 11, 1842, in the small Waldo County farming town of Morrill, Maine, and spent his life on the rocky uplands between Morrill and North Belmont, where the Meservey name had been established for generations. He worked the land as a hardscrabble mixed-husbandry farmer, raising livestock and field crops on ground better suited to pasture than tillage. He earned local renown as a stockman: at the Waldo County fairs of 1908 and 1910 he exhibited Durham (Shorthorn) cattle and working oxen — the heavy draft animals that still powered Maine farm work into the early twentieth century.

His later years were marked by hardship and controversy. In 1922, at the age of 79, he was tried for arson in connection with the burning of the Meservey homestead, a case in which his son Frank C. was also implicated — an episode that casts a complicated shadow over an otherwise ordinary farming life and likely reflected the financial strain pressing on many aging Maine farmers. He died March 31, 1923, in Morrill, only months after the trial. The trial's outcome remains only partly clear from the record, so details beyond the established dates and the fair and court notices should be treated with caution.

@I322454164375@.title: Eldora “Dora” (Meservey) Fernald
narrative: Eldora “Dora” Meservey was born in 1883 in Morrill, Maine, a daughter of the Waldo County farmer Daniel Smith Meservey. She grew up in the same Morrill and North Belmont farming neighbourhood her father worked, in a household shaped by the rhythms of dairy stock, oxen, and hayfields. In 1904 she married Medwin Fernald of neighbouring Troy, the wedding taking place in the shire town of Belfast — a common choice for couples from the inland Waldo County towns.

After marrying, Dora moved to the Fernald homestead in Troy, where she raised the next generation of the Troy Fernald family. Her son Clyde Fernald carried on the farm, and through him she became a grandmother of the Troy Fernald children — a later family obituary fondly names “Medwin and Dora” as the paternal grandparents who anchored the Troy household. Dora lived a long life rooted in that farm community, dying in 1968.

@I322454166501@.title: Abbie (Cromwell) Fernald
narrative: Abbie M. Cromwell was born in 1852 and became the wife of Joseph F. Fernald of Troy, Maine, a farming town in northern Waldo County. As a Fernald farm wife in the latter half of the nineteenth century, her world centred on the household economy of a Maine hill farm — gardening, dairy work, preserving, textile work, and raising children — labour essential to a farm's survival that the formal records of the period rarely captured by name.

Her lasting place in the family line is as the mother of Medwin Fernald, who would marry Dora Meservey of Morrill in 1904 and continue the family on the Troy homestead. Through that line Abbie stands at the head of the twentieth-century Troy Fernalds. She died in 1934. Her dates and central relationships are the firm points of her record; the narrative here is a modest synthesis consistent with the known facts and the typical experience of a Troy farm household of her generation.

@I322454165032@.title: Oscar Bragg of Albion
narrative: Oscar Blaine Bragg was born April 11, 1884, in Albion, Maine, a farming town in eastern Kennebec County; the local Albion genealogical records confirm his Albion birth and his place in the long-established Bragg family there. He spent his life as an Albion farmer and was active in family and community affairs — in 1918 he served as executor of the will of Alvin N. Bragg, a role signalling that he was regarded as a responsible, trusted member of his kin network. He married into the Fuller family; his wife is remembered as “Katie.”

A memorable local episode came in 1926, when a bear was caught in traps Oscar had set, drawing crowds to see the catch. Remarkably, he lived to about 100, dying in 1985 — a life that spanned from the horse-and-ox era of Maine farming nearly to the present. He was grandfather to the Troy Fernald children through his daughter Marion. The headline facts are documented; the finer details of the bear story and his later years rest on local-column recollection and are held loosely.

@I322454164892@.title: Marion (Bragg) Fernald
narrative: Marion F. Bragg was born June 22, 1909, in Albion, Maine, a daughter of the Albion farmer Oscar Blaine Bragg. She grew up among the closely connected Bragg and Fuller families, and appears in the town columns of the 1910s and 1920s visiting her grandmother, “Mrs. Fuller” — the kind of social note that filled Maine's weekly papers and confirms her place in that Albion kin network.

She married Clyde Fernald of Troy, son of Medwin and Dora (Meservey) Fernald, and moved to the Fernald homestead in Troy, where she helped run the family farm and raised her children. @I322625941849@.title: Joan (Hurst) Tilley — Mayflower
narrative: Joan Hurst was baptized at Henlow, Bedfordshire, England, on 13 March 1567/68, a daughter of William Hurst and Rose Marshe. She first married Thomas Rogers (no relation to the Mayflower passenger of that name) at Henlow, and after his death married John Tilley there on 20 September 1596. With John she had several children baptized at Henlow, the youngest being Elizabeth, born about 1607. In 1620 Joan, John, and their daughter Elizabeth sailed on the Mayflower to the New World, where John signed the Mayflower Compact.

Joan died during the Pilgrims' devastating first winter at Plymouth in early 1621 — the same season that claimed her husband and roughly half the Mayflower's company; the exact date is unrecorded. Their deaths left about thirteen-year-old Elizabeth orphaned; she was taken in by other colonists and later married fellow passenger John Howland, becoming an ancestor of millions. (The often-repeated detail of a secret night burial on Cole's Hill is traditional.)
